description_en: To make something active or operative; to start a process or function.
example: In immunology, the binding of an antigen to a B cell receptor can activate the B cell, leading to its proliferation and differentiation into plasma cells.
collocation: to activate, and activate, can activate

description_en: To have or own something; to have as a quality, characteristic, or attribute.
example: In many legal systems, the right to possess property is distinct from the right to own it, with possession often implying physical control rather than absolute title.
collocation: to possess, not possess, they possess

description_en: The subjects comprising a course of study in a school or college.
example: The university's revised curriculum emphasizes interdisciplinary approaches, integrating humanities with STEM fields to foster holistic critical thinking.
collocation: the curriculum, of curriculum, school curriculum

description_en: To move continuously or freely through a closed system or area; to spread or transmit widely.
example: In the human body, blood must continuously circulate through the arteries and veins to deliver oxygen and nutrients to all tissues.
collocation: to circulate, and circulate, not circulate

description_en: An aspect or feature of a situation, problem, or thing; also, a measurable extent of some kind, such as length, breadth, or height.
example: The study of quantum mechanics introduced a new dimension to our understanding of the universe, challenging classical physics paradigms.
collocation: the dimension, new dimension, a dimension
